elements.sent-2411-001: Numerical homogenization methods aim at providing appropriate coarse-scale approximations of solutions to (elliptic) partial differential equations that involve highly oscillatory coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-002: The localized orthogonal decomposition (LOD) method is an effective way of dealing with such coefficients, especially if they are non-periodic and non-smooth.
elements.sent-2411-003: It modifies classical finite element basis functions by suitable fine-scale corrections.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, we make use of the structure of the LOD method, but we propose to calculate the corrections based on a Deep Ritz approach involving a parametrization of the coefficients to tackle temporal variations or uncertainties.
elements.sent-2411-005: Numerical examples {for a parabolic model problem are presented to assess the performance of the approach.. Keywords.}
elements.sent-2411-006: Composite materials play an important role in modern technologies, as they enable the combination of complex properties such as high strength, lightweight, thermal resistance, and electrical conductivity- characteristics that are often unattainable with plain homogeneous materials.
elements.sent-2411-007: A notable example for their application can be found in batteries.
elements.sent-2411-008: An interesting question in this context is the study of the thermal behavior of the cell during the charging/discharging protocol, especially during fast charging, where increased voltages and currents can lead to thermal breakdowns if not properly monitored and controlled.
elements.sent-2411-009: In the most simple manner, the temperature in a battery cell can be modeled by a parabolic equation (similar to~\cite{Ves22 ).}
elements.sent-2411-010: More precisely, we seek the heat distribution $u$ that solves \begin{equation}\label{eq:model} \partial_t u(x, t)-\operatorname{div}(a (x,t) \nabla u(x,t)) = f(x,t), \qquad x \in \Omega, \quad t \in [0,T],\end{equation} where $\Omega$ is an open bounded domain, $T$ the final time, $a$ a given heat conductivity coefficient, and $f$ a heat source.
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\label{eq:model} \partial_t u(x, t)-\operatorname{div}(a (x,t) \nabla u(x,t)) = f(x,t), \qquad x \in \Omega, \quad t \in [0,T],\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-011: Note that $a$ is oscillatory in space and may change over time.
elements.sent-2411-012: In particular, battery cells usually consist of a large amount of layers that have very small widths $\varepsilon$.
elements.sent-2411-013: This multiscale structure makes the simulation using standard methods, such as the finite element method (FEM), inherently expensive.
elements.sent-2411-014: If $\varepsilon$ is not resolved by the mesh parameter, the solution behaves badly even if only macroscopic approximation properties are of interest.
elements.sent-2411-015: Therefore, very fine meshes are necessary to obtain reasonable results (this is also known as the pre-asymptotic behavior of the FEM).
elements.sent-2411-016: Thus, reducing the computational complexity with {tailor-made multiscale methods is highly beneficial.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Under additional assumptions on the coefficient~$a$ (such as periodicity and scale separation in~\cite{Ves22 ), the use of multiscale methods based on analytical homogenization theory are an appropriate and efficient choice.}
elements.sent-2411-018: The method applied by~\cite{Ves22 is the heterogeneous multiscale method (HMM), which has first been introduced in the elliptic setting in \cite{EE03,EE05 (see also \cite{AbdEEV12 ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: The methodology has since then been applied to various parabolic and parabolic-type problems, see, e.g.,~\cite{MinZ07,AbdV12,Abd16,AbdH17 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: It may also be used to tackle highly oscillatory coefficients in space and time and stochastic partial differential equations (PDEs), see~\cite{EckV23 and~\cite{AbdP12 , respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: The HMM calculates a coarse system matrix by approximating the homogenized coefficient from analytical homogenization theory.
elements.sent-2411-022: The system is then used to compute a coarse approximation of the (homogenized) solution to the original PDE.
elements.sent-2411-023: Another approach is the multiscale finite element method, see \cite{HouW97,AllB05 , which corrects coarse basis functions by solving operator-adapted problems in each element of the mesh.}
elements.sent-2411-024: The corresponding analysis is based on analytical homogenization theory as well, which again requires structural assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-025: In practice, the structure of materials in batteries is more involved, specifically the so-called active material is usually strongly heterogeneous as well as non-periodic.
elements.sent-2411-026: This promotes the use of more general approaches, so-called numerical homogenization techniques that provably work under minimal assumptions on the coefficient.
elements.sent-2411-027: A starting point for further developments has been the variational multiscale method introduced in~\cite{Hug95,HugFMQ98 , which decomposes the problem into an equation for a coarse-scale and a fine-scale function, respectively, and appropriately includes fine-scale information into the coarse-scale equation.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Further strategies include rough polyharmonic splines~\cite{OwhZB14 , where basis functions are constructed based on a {constrained minimization of the PDE operator in the $L^2$-norm, or gamblets~\cite{Owh17,OwhS19 , where the energy of basis functions is minimized.}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Gamblets are introduced based on a game theoretic approach to multiscale problems and have been applied to parabolic PDEs in~\cite{OwhZ17 .}
elements.sent-2411-030: Other approaches under minimal assumptions are generalized (multiscale) finite element methods~\cite{BabL11,EfeGH13,MaS22,MaSD22 that build basis functions based on locally solving spectral problems.}
elements.sent-2411-031: For a more in-depth overview of multiscale methods, we refer to~\cite{AltHP21 and the textbooks~\cite{OwhS19,MalP20 .}}
elements.sent-2411-032: The multiscale setting including space- and time-dependent coefficients poses difficult questions since the above-mentioned approaches require an update of the spatial approximation spaces at a relatively high cost.
elements.sent-2411-033: Methods that work around this bottleneck are thus vital and examples dealing with this question employing space-time approaches include an approach by~\cite{OwhZ07 , where the basis functions of the {ansatz and test space are subject to a global harmonic transform, or the high-dimensional sparse finite element method in~\cite{TanH19 , which requires a clear scale separation of the coefficient.}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: The construction of optimal local subspaces based on the generalized finite element method for space-time coefficients is analyzed in \cite{SchS22,SchST23 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Another space-time multiscale method based on the generalized multiscale finite element method is considered in~\cite{ChuELY18 .}
elements.sent-2411-036: The proposed method in this paper is based on the localized orthogonal decomposition (LOD) method introduced in~\cite{MalP14,HenP13 (see also~\cite{MalP20 ), that constructs an appropriate orthogonal decomposition of an ideal approximation space and remaining fine-scale functions.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: The ideal approximation space can be well-approximated by correcting classical coarse-scale finite element functions on a chosen scale~$H > \varepsilon$ with the solutions to local auxiliary problems (so-called corrections).
elements.sent-2411-038: The LOD method provably works under very general assumptions on the coefficient $a$, which makes it fitting to our problem.
elements.sent-2411-039: The approach has previously been applied to parabolic PDEs in~\cite{MalP18 and to coupled elliptic-parabolic problems in~\cite{MalP17,AltCMPP20 .}}
elements.sent-2411-040: The LOD method is particularly well-suited for time-dependent problems, where the modified approximation space is built in an offline phase.
elements.sent-2411-041: A substantial speedup is then achieved in the online phase when iterating through time as the resulting system matrices are of moderate size only.
elements.sent-2411-042: The offline phase where the LOD basis functions are set up is the bottleneck of the method.
elements.sent-2411-043: If the coefficient changes in time, substantial re-computations of the approximation space are necessary such that the method loses its efficiency in the online phase.
elements.sent-2411-044: In~\cite{LjuMM22 the time-dependence was tackled by posing the LOD method in a space-time setting.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Here, we stick to the idea of updating the approximation spaces but we avoid expensive re-computations by employing an appropriate trained artificial neural network (ANN) that realizes the corrections and is parametrized by the coefficient.
elements.sent-2411-046: This allows for quick updates once the coefficient has changed and an adjustment is necessary.
elements.sent-2411-047: The paper is structured as follows.
elements.sent-2411-048: In Section~\ref{sec:homogenization , we establish the parabolic model problem considered in this paper and introduce the basics of the LOD method as the spatial discretization technique.}
elements.sent-2411-049: In Section~\ref{sec:ann , we explain how to compute the Deep Ritz-based corrections, which are eventually used to set up a modified LOD approach.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Finally, we present numerical examples in Section~\ref{sec:examples .}

elements.sent-2411-051: Recall that we {aim at (approximately) solving parabolic PDEs of the form~\eqref{eq:model .}}

elements.section-2411-003: Approximation of the Corrections with Artifical Neural Networks
elements.subsection-2411-004: Definition of an artificial neural network
elements.sent-2411-117: Recall that we want to employ neural network models to approximate the correction resulting from the minimization problem~\eqref{eq:mincorr .}
elements.sent-2411-118: In particular, we consider a neural surrogate based on a feed-forward artificial neural network~(ANN), also known as multilayer perceptron (MLP).
elements.sent-2411-119: Such an ANN consists of a finite number of fully-connected layers, i.e., each unit of each layer, called ({artificial ) {neuron , is connected to every unit of the subsequent layer.}}
elements.sent-2411-120: The number of neurons in each layer is known as the width of a layer, which is finite in practice.
elements.sent-2411-121: Note that some theoretical results require the consideration of the limiting case, i.e., infinitely wide networks, see for instance \cite{jacot2018neural .}
elements.sent-2411-122: The first and last layer of an ANN are referred to as input and output layers, respectively, whereas the intermediate ones are known as hidden layers.
elements.sent-2411-123: The depth of a neural network typically refers to the number of layers excluding the input layer~\cite{goodfellow2016deep .}
elements.sent-2411-124: Figure~\ref{fig:nn depicts the structure and connections of this type of ANN.}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.75\textwidth]{images/neuralNetwork_vrg} \caption{Illustration of a fully-connected feed-forward artificial neural network. The neurons are depicted as circles, and the connections represented by black arrows.}\label{fig:nn}
elements.sent-2411-125: Neural network models can be understood as input/output systems that are governed by a collection of simple rules at each neuron of each layer.
elements.sent-2411-126: It is applied component-wise, see \cite{pinkus1999approximation,goodfellow2016deep for more details.}
elements.sent-2411-127: Popular choices of the activation function include the hyperbolic tangent, the sigmoid function or rectified linear unit.
elements.sent-2411-128: The a~priori unknown coefficients of the affine transformations correspond to the trainable parameters of the network.

elements.sent-2411-152: When training neural networks, the goal is typically to find the set of parameters $\btheta$ that minimizes an empirical loss function.

elements.sent-2411-285: We have presented a numerical homogenization strategy based on the LOD method using a Deep Ritz approach for computing the corrections to leverage generalization properties of neural networks to, e.g., overcome the bottleneck of re-computations in the case of time-dependence or uncertainties in the PDE coefficient.
elements.sent-2411-286: This work is supposed to be a proof of concept regarding the replacement of the correction by a neural network-based ansatz function that is minimized with a constraint condition.
elements.sent-2411-287: In particular, it is trained based on an additional parametrization to eventually allow for quick computations of required corrections based on a given coefficient.
elements.sent-2411-288: We have presented two examples to illustrate the performance of the approach.
elements.sent-2411-289: A straight-forward extensions is the amplification of the Deep Ritz method using different ANN architectures.
elements.sent-2411-290: A more technical modification would be a suitable addition of the localization parameter to the training parameters.
elements.sent-2411-291: Finally, a rigorous a posteriori analysis is still open.. \newcommand{\etalchar [1]{$^{#1}$}.}
